summary: DHH celebrates that HEY email can finally be purchased directly on the iPhone in the US, following a years-long battle with Apple over their 30% App Store commission and control of customer relationships. He credits Epic Games' $100M+ lawsuit and Judge Gonzalez Rogers' ruling for forcing Apple to allow developers to link to external payment systems. The post recounts the awkward workarounds HEY was forced to use, including bizarre burner accounts, after refusing Apple's in-app purchase demands. DHH frames this as a near-total victory while urging Apple to accept the ruling worldwide and return to competing on product quality rather than platform taxation.
key_points: Epic Games spent over $100M suing Apple, and Apple's possibly criminal contempt of court ultimately forced the resolution allowing developers to link to external payment systems
The fight was about more than the 30% cut — retaining the direct customer relationship for refunds and multi-platform billing was equally important to 37signals
Apple's App Store restrictions created countless customer-hostile experiences, including inability to buy Kindle books or sign up for Netflix subscriptions within apps
The ruling currently only applies to the US store, and DHH hopes Apple will voluntarily extend it worldwide or that European regulators will adopt a similar mechanism
DHH expresses hope that Apple will pivot back to competing on product quality rather than relying on platform lock-in and developer taxation
money_quotes: It was the kind of test that calls you to account for what you believe and asks what you're willing to risk to see it through.
The 30% toll gets all the attention, and it is ludicrously egregious, but to us, it's just as much about retaining that direct customer relationship.
Apple's obsession with squeezing every last dollar from developers has produced countless customer-hostile experiences on the iPhone.
Thanks to Apple's gag orders, developers were not even allowed to explain the confusing situation.

We've always been happy to pay Apple for hosting our application on the App Store. But being forced to hand over 30% of the business, as well as the direct customer relationship, was always an unacceptable overreach.
I really want Apple to return to being a shining city on the hill. To show that you can win in the market merely by making better products.
They don't need these gangster extortion techniques to make the numbers that Cook has promised Wall Street.
They've lost their way and moral compass over the last half decade or so, but that's only one leadership pivot away from being found again.
key_insight: Apple's App Store monopoly was broken not by regulation or market forces but by one company willing to spend $100M on litigation and a judge willing to hold Apple in contempt — and the result is a fairer marketplace where developers can maintain direct customer relationships.
